findings.reachability: Any unauthenticated visitor can supply ?lang=xx in the URL. The value is used as the current language when neither WPML nor Polylang is active but a third-party filter sets the provider to something other than 'not set'. The language value then flows into getPermalink() which passes it to the wpml_permalink filter, potentially redirecting users via an attacker-supplied language tag.
findings.justification: Line 132 reads $_GET['lang'] directly without a nonce or capability check; isLangCode() only enforces character class and length (2-15 alpha chars per segment), which does not prevent the value being used to manipulate URL generation through WPML's wpml_permalink filter in getPermalink().

detection_pattern.description: Detects direct reads of a 'lang' parameter from superglobals without sanitization. In multilingual WordPress plugins, unsanitized lang values can influence URL generation, cache keys, or database lookups, and may enable open redirects or cache-poisoning when the value flows into permalink or redirect functions.

findings.reachability: Internal (server-side only). The table name is built from $wpdb->prefix (WordPress-controlled) concatenated with the hardcoded string 'icl_translations'. The $postType and $taxonomy parameters are sanitized with sanitize_key() before use as %s placeholders inside wpdb->prepare(). Not exploitable in isolation.
findings.justification: wpdb->prepare() does not escape table or column names; interpolating any variable into the SQL string before prepare() creates a risky pattern that future maintainers may copy with attacker-controlled input. Both call sites suppress the phpcs warning (WordPress.DB.PreparedSQL.InterpolatedNotPrepared) which masks the pattern from automated tooling.

detection_pattern.section_name: Table name variable interpolated inside wpdb->prepare()
detection_pattern.description: Catches patterns where a variable table name is string-interpolated into the SQL passed to wpdb->prepare(). prepare() only parameterises %s/%d/%f placeholders; a variable table name bypasses all escaping. Dangerous when the table-name variable is derived from user input or a filter value.
